---
title: "Journal Entry 5: Chapters 3 and 4 Reflections"
date: "2026-04-01"
categories: [reflection, writing, methodology, results]
description: "Reflections on writing the Methodology and Results chapters"
---

![Methodology and results reflection](../thumbnail.jpg)

## Reflections on Writing Chapters 3 and 4
Writing Chapters 3 (Methodology) and 4 (Experimental Results) required
translating an experimental idea into an implementable harness. The
methodology focused on engineering decisions: implementing OPENCODE as
a local subagent (file-driven manifests), using AST-aware edits so
mutations remain syntactically valid. Choosing
`pytest` as the primary validator made the evaluation concrete and
easy to automate, and `mutmut` provided optional mutation-coverage
support.

Challenges: describing the toolchain concisely was surprisingly
difficult—readers need enough detail to re-run experiments, but not so
much that the chapter becomes an installation guide. I addressed this
by including clear reproduction steps and a small example manifest.

On experiments: I designed the runs to answer the three research
questions (detection, repair, effort) and to measure metrics such as
detection rate, repair success rate, iterations-to-repair, and
time-to-repair. The illustrative numerical-integration workload made it
possible to inject many deterministic mutations and observe repair
behavior, though the workload's small size limits generalization.

Key lessons from the Results chapter:

- Tests drive outcomes: when unit tests fully capture intended
	behavior, agents tend to propose focused fixes; when tests are weak,
	agents can overfit and produce patches that pass tests but are
	semantically incomplete.
- Cost matters: repeated propose-apply-test cycles mean test runtime
	dominates evaluation cost; incremental test-caching or sandboxing can
	substantially reduce runtime for large studies.

Overall, these chapters made the project concrete and taught me the
importance of designing experiments that are both reproducible and
cost-aware.
